item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason, the phrase "nothing in this book is a metaphor" has stuck with me harder than any other phrase in the rest of the SCP mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy +1000 to a legend

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Someone on the prior page gave this link, which I think ties is really well with the themes and events of the article, also tying fifthism into Britain's occult history.

Like Masque says, it might also be a signal that the author is privy the secrets of their inner circle (which they probably aren't), given that Crowley's shit was always about exclusiveness and secrecy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Utterly terrifying in the best possible way.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is basically Bush-era Tlön, Uqbar, Orbis Tertius and everything that implies. Also this article reads like how circa-2006 news footage looks. I can taste the rotten South Beach Diet compost every time I read this.

A classic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might also be a reference to the old Christian song "Do You Hear What I Hear."

Given the other references to stars, the EID director might be pointing a finger at something the Fifth Church higher-ups know that they keep secret from the average follower.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On re-read I got curious about the "A*A*" threat that Director Erlenmeyer ended with.
So I plugged it into Google and got some tantalizing results.

A* - read as "A star", is a common computer pathfinding algorithm. So the doubling "A*A*" could be a coded way of saying, "path to a star". In other words, "I know where you live."

Sagittarius A* - is an astronomical radio source, most likely a supermassive black hole, in the center of the Milky Way. There's a fridge logic here that a black hole is the antithesis of a star, and the Fifthists are essentially praying to particular stars; so the Director could be invoking their version of the devil. Either just to mess with their screwy cult-warped heads, or because he actually has a black hole based doomsday weapon that could annihilate even smoke-forms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The celebrity parts really scared me, and the overall story is just so good! It's definetely amongst my favourites. The only problem is I can only give one vote.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Highly satirical to pop cultures and ducking cults and still has the creepy scp smell to it
LOVE it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fifth fifth fifth fifth fifth

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unlike nearly all books of the “self-help” genre, SCP-1425 is directly efficacious in assisting the subject in achieving personal goals.

I always enjoy reading this line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, I love this article so much. I come back to read it every so often because it's just so damn good.

Peppy: Alright, kids, I think you’ll be surprised by our next guest. He’s a clown that teaches as well as he learns, and dishes it out as well as he takes it! Because nobody hates…!

Also, I see what you did there. ;)

item.title: Simple and small -> terrible and complex
item.content:encoded:
I think this shows the secret to many great SCP's. starting as small and innoculous as you can, but then growing and growing and becoming more and more terrifying.

I keep clicking upvote ut it will only take one…much be a voting glitch because I'm SURE I need to upvote more than once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since I don't think anyone's brought it up yet, I found this article the other day and it seems to be what
"A * A *" is referencing. Interesting thematic allusion of sorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A prime example of what an SCP can be.

All others should aspire to write something as genuinely unsettling and well thought out as this article.

Easiest +1 I've ever given, and by far my favorite of all time.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Since crosslink was already implemented, the 'crosslink' tag is removed. Please remember to remove it when the edits have been made.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I would be very careful to not imply that anything that isn't Fifthist is connected to Star Signals. That said there is a lot of Fifthist stuff that could be linked here.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Alright, will remove the non fifth church edit. Thanks for telling me!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of the crosslink to the non-Fifthist article, but the Fifth Church link is fine. I agree with sirpudding that sticking to crosslinks with things that are specifically tied in with the Fifth Church and 1425 would be better.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Thanks a bunch for the suggestions. Will add more edits.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Well the soul smoke parts could link to SCP-1523 and or SCP-1724. Obviously the Fifthist Hub could be linked nearly anywhere.

More ambitiously Dust Brothers and Constellation Starfish could be inserted into the event log with the other celebrities.

There's probably other obvious places that the Fifthist stuff intersects here, so it might be worth going through everything tagged "fifthist" and see what connections with Star Signals already are there.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Possibly! Any suggestions?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't links to more explicitly fifthist things be more relevant?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer stopped. Edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ show block
– hide block

I propose the following edit:

Some helpful advice that will save you in your coming weeks:

  • Mirrors are for other people.
  • Sit in a dark room by yourself for at least an hour per day. Move around as much as you’re made to.
  • If you feel yourself developing a soul, go outside immediately and follow the direction of the smoke until you meet them.
  • Always listen for the sound slon of trwoll plr mlgn thei.
  • Love the archons. When you hate them, they see you.

To this:

Some helpful advice that will save you in your coming weeks:

  • Mirrors are for other people.
  • Sit in a dark room by yourself for at least an hour per day. Move around as much as you’re made to.
  • If you feel yourself developing a soul, go outside immediately and follow the direction of the smoke until you meet them.
  • Always listen for the sound slon of trwoll plr mlgn thei.
  • Love the archons. When you hate them, they see you.

SCP-1425 is believed to have been written and published by operatives of the Fifth Church, an influential

To this:

SCP-1425 is believed to have been written and published by operatives of the Fifth Church, an influential

item.title: Inspiration?
item.content:encoded: To the author, can I ask what inspired you to write this? Where the ideas came from? Based on your user picture you seem to be a comic fan and some of what is in this SCP seems familiar. In a good way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this article. The rabbit hole goes deep. The smoke part was a great story. I FELT it inside. Deeper than the wells of pursuit they've dug for your longing. It's like a forged identity, or a mass of faceless friends, they just don't listen until you've become the numbers.

My belief is one of a multitude of fair-feeling iullths. I don't condone this. I don't condone this. He's the grace we need in a time so muddy and green. Like hair off the backs of second-hand starvationaries, it feels nice when it gets in your brain, doesn't it? It stays there. It stays there. It stays there.

Be a proDUCt of this Time, or she WILL birth it. There's nothing to it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does EID stand for Esoteric Intelligence Division?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beck: Why don’t I just say… I think we’ll stay together.

I'm not sure which came first, but is this meant to reflect/reflected by the unknown singer's "Baby, if we can stay together" in 1982? Because I never felt right about the way I did that in my reading, and seeing something similar in this gave me chills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
guys I'm scared I found a Star Signals NSFW

https://twitter.com/superdeluxe/status/683365577914580992

item.title: Re: A brief Summary of why I love this SCP
item.content:encoded: Seconded. Thirded?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know after reading this again, I cant bring myself to downvote this. There are just too many good things I enjoyed about it.

Please excuse the prior rant, I was "suffering from exhaustion" at the time. Yes, "pretentious" may seem redundant but it was the closest thing to another phrase I was thinking of in my native language at the time so I just went with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since pretentious is a word used for literally anything, what do you actually mean? Cause I can't tell what you're trying to say that you haven't already said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was absolutely incredible until the final E-peening addendum which was ham-fisted, , overly expository and completed destroyed any suspension of disbelief as well as the overall tone of the article. All it did was draw further attention to how impossible it really would have been to contain this event. Very disappointing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-342 has 5987 words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I realize that this is pretty old, but I've just read it recently and noticed one minor nitpick:
The back jacket copy calls it a "novel" but it clearly isn't written as a fictional narrative. It probably should just say "book" in context.

item.title: A little confused
item.content:encoded:
This article was clearly very well-written and a lot of work was put into it, but I'm having a little trouble understanding it. Don't get me wrong; that's sort of what this site's writing style is like to me at times (such that I wish there was a simple version of these articles, like Wikipedia has) so I usually look to the discussion pages, only to mostly feel bad that I didn't get it.

Essentially, it's a self-help book that grants reality-warping abilities to those that read it. I understand that and I find it perfectly creepy. What I'm a little frustrated at is what some of the events in the Event Log really mean. Infected suffer from this "Ojai syndrome" but what does it do? It grants abilities but it also seems to make people more murderous or just act insane? Some of the TV broadcasts seemed a little too cryptic to me. I need a little help here.

Is there also something more concerning the stars referred to that may imply something paranormal about them? I.e., were the stars the source of some of these strange occurrences or were they simply "practices?" I remember Djoric having mentioned this article as part of their inspiration for the angry "star gods" in their canon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is still one of my favourite skips of all time

Unlike nearly all books of the “self-help” genre, SCP-1425 is directly efficacious in assisting the subject in achieving his or her goals.

and this is my favourite sentence in the entire article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just noting that I reverted it back to pre- Duck Stuff status. Apologies if I made a bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is funny and everything, but can I please have my favorite Fifthist Church SCP back?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and now I have theme of duck tales stuck in my head again.

Also this is so awesome and extensive I feel you should keep today's version around as -j, but I may just be really nostalgic over darkwing duck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ducks have aligned, and the duck signal has been sent. Quack quack.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I forgot to upvote this.

This deserves so much more credit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way late here, but I think I figured this one out:

Interviewer: Ask them about the machine elves sometime. See how many they can name.
MP: At this point? Goodness, no. They couldn’t name a Dero that killed their own parents.

Obvious thing is obvious: these two believe that Deros and machine elves existed, and were "undone". And that everyone who doesn't remember them has had their memories/history screwed with.

Except these two are the wrong ones, the influence of the scip, and their belief that things could be "undone" (changed), made them think that anyone who thought they never happened was wrong — reality didn't change one bit, but to those under the scip's influence, their beliefs about reality did.

That is, as far as actually changing history is concerned, it wasn't bending reality, it was just a mindfuck…which, if you get enough people to believe it…well, the world was never in color, that we think it always was is because of the Foundation covering up a scip (an -EX one). Because history is written by the victor, even if that means the victor of a worldwide mindfuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: was gonna jump on the bandwagon until the addendum, but now im ehh about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, hot damn, this is fucking fantastic. The excerpts from the book and the quotes from infected citizens were legitimately unsettling, rather than being cheesy CRAAAAZY talk. The events escalated beautifully and logically (I, for one, have no issue with believing that the Foundation could erase the existence of a book from civilian knowledge), and I adore all the chilling things it insinuated. It's amazing that you could take something that, in essence, was a generic magic item and create a bone-chilling series of events that nearly rewrote history. This is just magnificent throughout. Brilliant work, Silber.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I read it the same as staunchly, which is to say, different (maybe even opposite?) than how you read it, Devilot. The book was undoing events/things that definitely already occurred in our reality. The interviewers initial statement in Event Log 1425-05 seems to confirm this for me: "Interviewer: You know, there are some people out there who believe that, if we wish something didn’t happen hard enough, it really didn’t happen." I take that to mean that as a result of the book, people were causing past events to "unhappen" (become undone?) from reality. I'm not sure though why these two individuals were (at that point, anyway) still retaining memories of "unhappened" events.

This is a great, great SCP - by far one of my favs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh, this is just so good. Such a standout example of why this site is one of the highest quality, most interesting things on the internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm misunderstanding your post, but the implication, as I read it seems to be that the book was writing us into a universe where those things did happen, for whatever reason, not that it was writing us away from a prior reality where they didn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I adore the depth and detail of this; I also appreciate the implication that they rewrote things that we accept as fact (e.g. Gandhi not committing public suicide). Great creep. I've read all the other comments on plausibility, but it really doesn't stretch my credulity that much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Super creepy. Loved the descriptions of the distortions - the "hole inside you" and "manipulating reality around you" as if to suggest that you can physically reach out and twist spacetime in multiple dimensions beyond those we know. The idea that just knowing this information gives you the power to reshape all of reality, but that doing so basically just turns it into some sort of extradimensional hell. The descriptions of the black smoke emitting from peoples' insides, the "world being slaughtered like a cat", the way they casually talk about these things ("hey, why hasn't your prime minister been eaten?" like it's just ordinary smalltalk), the whole idea that someone published this book probably intending for all this to happen. The description of how London had been "torn apart and sewn back together" especially. It all just sounds so wrong.

Easily one of my favourite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's way past the fact but this is my favourite SCP so far, the dialogue is brilliant.

item.title: Re: A brief Summary of why I love this SCP
item.content:encoded: That's because I agree with you.

item.title: Re: A brief Summary of why I love this SCP
item.content:encoded: Huh. I was kind of expecting someone to disagree or at least insult me for my position by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this SPC. For all its faults, Paula Abdul vomiting up her soul while Randy Jackson and Ryan Seacrest look on with disgust and disinterest is just a delightfully, horribly, soul-scarringly brilliant. +1 one for you, you sick, sick puppy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to love this one with all my heart, and the chronicling and the descriptions of the TV broadcasts and such reek beautifully of aspects of "Ronald Reagan Cut Up While Talking", and the thing at the end helped to explain SOME lingering questions.

But ultimately…. just ultimately… with some heavy strain and re-thinking…

I just… don't… get it.

There seemed to be no pattern or intent to the effects, beyond "random creepy/offputting shit and mindless jibbering", unless there was some intentional pattern that I missed or was excised and I missed the indication of its excision.

While I always find it fun to see random creepy, it would be much more convincing and memorable if there's some manner of pattern to it, even if it's just a token hotfix explanation like "it wasn't properly executed for human minds, like putting a Playstation game disc into an Xbox system and forcing it to play the disc as best it can."

Still upvoting, and hoping that if I let this linger a bit, Fridge Logic or a re-read will give me an answer, but thus far, it seems too random to me to be one of those "new instant classic" type dealies like SCP-1981 or 1733 or 1370

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this one. My only small gripe with (which may be entirely imagined) is that I feel like it needs the greater context provided by some of the other Fifth Church-Related SCPs in order to really work.

Again, minor gripe which you may just chalk up to the completionist in me speaking. Regardless, this is a +1 for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's supposed to be a reference to something potentially damaging to the Fifthists or their reputation that they're keeping under wraps, and which he just revealed to any spies to undermine them. Beyond that, no real idea, and it's probably supposed to be cryptic.

item.title: A brief Summary of why I love this SCP
item.content:encoded:
I think I discovered the site during the so-called lolFoundation era, and although there was a lot bad about it, what I liked about that era, and don't like about the new era is the Foundation feels too impersonal and effective to be truly scary. One of the things that always got to me was the clear indication that the Foundation was staffed by actual people. Fallible people. People who sometimes made mistakes, or cracked stupid jokes to relieve the tension of what they had to deal with. People who were tasked with containing inexplicable things and preventing the end of the world, and, when you got right down to it, were no more psychologically prepared for that task than anyone else in their position would be.

Too often, the modern Foundation seems too faceless, too inhuman, too infallible for the fact that they're dealing with all this stuff to be really scary — even if their methods are questionable, they'll have it under control and safe from causing harm if it's remotely possible. As such, this is my favorite SCP of the modern era, because it doesn't deal with a foundation that's faceless and inhuman and flawless — it deals with one that's just as fallible as it ever was back in the day, one that nearly lost everything and succeeded mostly through luck, and suggests that the modern face of the Foundation is a comforting lie in front of an organization that's still deeply flawed and still at risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone were to make a movie or write a full novel about the SCP foundation, 1425 would be a perfect choice. This has replaced 093 as my favorite on this site, and I'll be shocked as all else if anything will top this. Beautifully horrifying; allow me to toss another +1 on the well-deserved pile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright, kids, I think you’ll be surprised by our next guest. He’s a clown that teaches as well as he learns, and dishes it out as well as he takes it! Because nobody hates…Bobble the Clown!

We're all going to die.
